items.title: GE Power India: Guidance -- Concall Update The order book of INR1,628 crore provides visibility for close to two years of execution from continuing op...
items.description_html:
GE Power India: Guidance -- Concall Update

The order book of INR1,628 crore provides visibility for close to two years of execution from continuing operations. Core services orders are short cycle, with 85% to 90% expected to be executed within 12 months.

The company expects and is working towards growth in core services, aiming to increase its current 18% market share in the INR4,000 crore targeted fleet.

The normalized EBITDA for FY26 was 11% at the entity level, and this base is set to be at par for future years.

The demerger of the Durgapur facility is targeted for closure within the current calendar year, with an expectation of within 12 months from March 31, 2026.

Priorities include driving disciplined execution, accelerating cash conversion, sustaining profitability, strengthening the core service franchise, and improving margins.

items.title: Tata Power: Guidance -- Concall Update Management acknowledged that FY '26 capital expenditure (CapEx) of INR 13,000 crores was below the previously g...
items.description_html:
Tata Power: Guidance -- Concall Update

Management acknowledged that FY '26 capital expenditure (CapEx) of INR 13,000 crores was below the previously guided range of INR 20,000-22,000 crores, primarily due to phasing impacts from delayed large utility-scale solar/wind and transmission line projects.

For FY '27, the company expects CapEx to be around INR 25,000 crores, including deferred projects from FY '26, and plans to add approximately 2 GW of IPP-RE capacity, with solar contributing 1.5 GW to 1.8 GW.

Co's manufacturing unit will supply cells and modules for its internal pipeline, including rooftop solar, which is expected to grow by at least 50-60% in FY '27, and utility-scale projects requiring Indian-made components from June 1, 2026.

Odisha DISCOMs are projected to have their "best year" in FY '27, with AT&C losses in other circles expected to reduce by approximately 2% annually, aiming for 12-13% within four to five years.

Management expects Tata Projects to return to profitability in FY '27 after resolving legacy project issues, and is exploring new coal-fired power plant opportunities if tariffs are attractive and PPAs are bankable.

items.title: Constrained Copper Supply: Key Mines and Normalization Timelines Cobre Panamá (First Quantum): Fully offline since late 2023 due to political and leg...
items.description_html:
Constrained Copper Supply: Key Mines and Normalization Timelines

Cobre Panamá (First Quantum): Fully offline since late 2023 due to political and legal disputes; stockpile processing expected to resume in 2H 2026, with full restart not anticipated before 1H 2027.

Grasberg (Freeport-McMoRan): Operating at reduced rates following a September 2025 underground mud-rush; expected to reach 65% capacity in 2H 2026 and 100% by end-2027.

Kamoa-Kakula (Ivanhoe/Zijin): Production constrained due to a 2025 seismic event; expected recovery to exceed 500 kt from 2028 onward.

El Teniente and Chuquicamata (Codelco): Facing seismic and transition-related challenges, with gradual normalization expected through 2026–2027 and beyond.

Overall, Jefferies emphasizes that supply growth will be slow, constrained by depletion, grade declines, and execution risks, while demand continues to strengthen across electrification and decarbonization themes.

items.title: BREAKING: US Federal government employment fell by -9,000 jobs in April, to 2.66 million, the lowest since May 1996. This marks the 14th monthly decli...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: US Federal government employment fell by -9,000 jobs in April, to 2.66 million, the lowest since May 1996.

This marks the 14th monthly decline over the last 15 months.

Since January 2025, the federal government has cut -12% of its headcount, or -345,000 jobs.

Federal jobs now account for 1.68% of total employment, the lowest percentage since at least the 1930s.

Meanwhile, the federal job openings rate fell -0.5 percentage points in March, to 2.7%, the 2nd-lowest since the 2020 pandemic low.

As a result, there were only 75,000 available vacancies in the sector, the 2nd-lowest since May 2020.

The federal workforce is contracting.

items.title: BREAKING: Copper prices have surged to a record $6.58 per pound, now up +75% since October 2023 and over +40% in 12 months. The surge comes amid tight...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Copper prices have surged to a record $6.58 per pound, now up +75% since October 2023 and over +40% in 12 months.

The surge comes amid tight supply, declining inventories in China, and surging demand for data center construction.

Furthermore, supply disruptions at the world's 2nd-largest copper mine in Indonesia are adding to the pressure.

Meanwhile, China's exports jumped +14% YoY in April, led by booming clean-tech shipments, components that are materially copper-intensive, tightening the market even further.

The global economy is scrambling for copper.

items.title: The US bond market crisis is intensifying. While everyone is focused on AI and the Iran War, the US bond market is in a complete meltdown. The 30Y Yie...
items.description_html:
The US bond market crisis is intensifying.

While everyone is focused on AI and the Iran War, the US bond market is in a complete meltdown.

The 30Y Yield is now above 5.00% and the 10Y Yield is nearing the pivotal 4.50% level, which resulted in President Trump's "90-day tariff pause" in April 2025.

Long-term yields are now ABOVE levels seen prior to Fed rate cuts in another brutal reminder that the Fed can not contain the long-end of the yield curve.

At the current pace, we will likely see US mortgage rates rise back above 7.00% this year.

The question then becomes:

How much longer can markets (or the US government) ignore the yield crisis?

And, who folds first?

items.description_html:
BCA || K-shaped economy now extends to business investment.

Demand for information processing equipment is growing even faster than it did in 1999, while demand for everything else has only kept pace with inflation for the past dozen years. The same divide now runs through business investment, profitability, and equity performance, all increasingly dominated by a narrow group of tech and tech-adjacent industries.

That concentration matters for global equity allocation. The durability of the tech capex boom (especially data center spending) will determine when investors should rotate away from the tech-dominated US in developed markets, and Korea and Taiwan in emerging markets.

items.title: PARKHOSPS delivered strong Q4 FY26 with 30.12% revenue growth and 46.63% PAT growth, driven by capacity expansion to 3,960 beds and improved occupancy...
items.description_html:
PARKHOSPS delivered strong Q4 FY26 with 30.12% revenue growth and 46.63% PAT growth, driven by capacity expansion to 3,960 beds and improved occupancy. EBITDA margin expanded 267 bps QoQ to 27.66%, meeting management's long-term target.

The balance sheet transformed post-IPO with debt-to-equity improving to 0.11x, while cash conversion remained strong at 1.20x CFO/PAT. Integration of four acquisitions is ongoing with initial losses, but capacity expansion trajectory positions the company well for sustained growth.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 30.12% YoY to ₹4,604.13 Mn in Q4, accelerating from 17.8% in Q3

PAT grew 46.63% YoY to ₹767.78 Mn in Q4

EBITDA margin expanded 267 bps QoQ to 27.66%, meeting the ~27% target

Operating leverage of 720 bps as expenses grew 22.9% vs revenue 30.1%

Debt-to-equity improved from 0.56x to 0.11x post-IPO

CFO/PAT at 1.20x indicating high earnings quality

Bed capacity expanded to 3,960 beds with Panchkula
launch

Risk Factors

Other expenses grew 35.6% YoY, faster than revenue, representing margin pressure

Acquired entities contributed loss before tax of ₹55.99 Mn indicating integration phase

Occupancy at 65% still below industry benchmarks of 70-75%

FY26 EBITDA margin at 26.45% slightly below 27% target

items.title: Neuland Laboratories delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 results with revenue up 136.4% YoY and FY26 revenue growing 37% to ₹2,023 Cr, significantly exceed...
items.description_html:
Neuland Laboratories delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 results with revenue up 136.4% YoY and FY26 revenue growing 37% to ₹2,023 Cr, significantly exceeding the 20-25% CAGR guidance.

EBITDA margin expanded 650 bps to 28.4%, PAT grew 40% to ₹364 Cr, and the company announced ₹143.4 Cr capacity expansion. However, free cash flow turned negative due to heavy capex and working capital buildup, representing a key monitoring point.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 37.0% YoY to ₹2,022.99 Cr, exceeding 20-25% CAGR guidance

PAT grew 40.0% YoY to ₹363.99 Cr with normalized

PAT up 79.7% YoY excluding exceptional items

EBITDA margin expanded 650 bps YoY to 28.4%

Operating leverage visible with expenses growing 27.5% vs revenue 37.0%

USA and Europe drove growth with 69.0% and 57.4% YoY respectively

Capacity expansion announced: 120.5 KL at ₹143.4 Cr

New director appointment: Dr. Maurizio Futran with 40+ years Big Pharma experience

Risk Factors

Free cash flow turned negative at ₹5,045 Lakh due to heavy capex and working capital

CFO/PAT ratio declined to 0.95x indicating weaker earnings quality

Working capital outflow of ₹13,162 Lakh driven by
inventory and receivables buildup

Trade receivables grew 71.5% vs revenue 37.0%, potential working capital pressure

Finance costs up 188.6% YoY driven by increased borrowings for capex

items.title: RT ValueEquity: The period of competitive advantage has shortened dramatically all over the world , Sanjoy Bhattacharya In my opinion I think this whe...
items.description_html:
RT ValueEquity
The period of competitive advantage has shortened dramatically all over the world , Sanjoy Bhattacharya

In my opinion I think this when it comes to MOAT's and in most cases assigning a higher terminal value to MOAT's may actually put you at a risk of a large de rating event when you cant forecast it , leading to irrational multiples defined by moats , until that one day it gets broken , because the rate of change and disruption to conventional MOAT's may be changing faster than ever

Some MOAT's deserve higher multiples , but we cannot assume it to perpetuity that those hold , 30 years back MOAT's were very difficult to break , now democratization of capital , tech , AI and constraints have actually turned more accessible , and we can already see conventional business models may face that terminal value risk !

video source:@moneycontrolcom

items.title: Borosil Renewables delivered a strong turnaround in Q4 FY26 with revenue growth of 17.8% YoY and EBITDA margin expansion of 1,140 bps to 32.8%, beatin...
items.description_html:
Borosil Renewables delivered a strong turnaround in Q4 FY26 with revenue growth of 17.8% YoY and EBITDA margin expansion of 1,140 bps to 32.8%, beating management's 28-30% margin guidance.

The company swung from loss to profit with normalized PAT of ₹380.52 Cr (up 1,037% YoY), driven by operating leverage, pricing power from anti-dumping duties, and significant debt reduction. Capacity expansion to 1,600 TPD by December 2026 provides a clear catalyst for revenue growth beyond Q4 FY27.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 17.8% YoY to ₹1,555.84 Cr with 33.7% YoY growth in Q4

EBITDA margin expanded to 32.8% in Q4 from 21.4% in Q4 FY25 (1,140 bps YoY)

PAT turned positive at ₹127.40 Cr in FY26 from loss of ₹86.97 Cr in FY25

Operating leverage of 20.8 percentage points (revenue +33.7% vs expenses +12.9%)

Finance costs declined 53.7% YoY to ₹335.59 Lakhs in Q4

Debt reduced 35.3% YoY to ₹162.14 Cr with debt/equity improving to 0.11x

Free cash flow positive at ₹227.14 Cr despite ₹197.37 Cr capex

Normalized PAT at ₹380.52 Cr (up 1,037% YoY) after exceptional items write-off

Capacity utilization at ~95% with 600 TPD expansion on track for December 2026

Anti-dumping duties effective till December 2029 providing pricing power

Risk Factors

Export revenue declined 31% YoY to ₹12.32 Cr in Q4 due to weak EU/Turkey/USA markets

Capacity constraint limiting revenue growth - running at 95% utilization

Export markets face weak demand and pricing pressure from East Asian competition

GMB/Geosphere fully written off at ₹359.78 Cr with no recovery expected

Q4 FY26 tax expense negative at ₹4,900.41 Lakhs due to deferred tax benefit (non-recurring)

Working capital: receivables increased ₹19.37 Cr outflow

items.title: V-Guard delivered a strong Q4 with revenue up 14.1% YoY and PAT up 23.1% YoY, validating summer demand expectations. Electronics (22.3% YoY) and Elect...
items.description_html:
V-Guard delivered a strong Q4 with revenue up 14.1% YoY and PAT up 23.1% YoY, validating summer demand expectations. Electronics (22.3% YoY) and Electricals (15.9% YoY) drove growth with margin expansion in Electricals to 12.3%.

The company achieved debt-free status with ICRA upgrading to AA+. However, full-year margin contracted 70 bps and Consumer Durables remains weak at 1.6% margin. The path to ₹9,000-10,000 crore revenue target by FY30 requires accelerating to 15-20% CAGR from current 7% growth rate.

Positive Factors

Q4 revenue grew 14.1% YoY to ₹1,755.27 crore with strong seasonal summer demand

Q4 PAT grew 23.1% YoY to ₹112.13 crore, outpacing revenue growth

Electronics segment grew 22.3% YoY with margin at 17.4% within guidance range

Electricals margin expanded to 12.3% from 11.4% FY average, strongest in two years

Sunflame recovered to +8.6% YoY after two quarters of decline

Company achieved debt-free status with borrowings at ₹8.84 crore

ICRA upgraded credit rating to AA+ from AA

Operating cash flow strong at ₹458.55 crore, covering PAT 1.49x

Q4 EBITDA margin expanded 40 bps YoY to 9.7%

Risk Factors

Full year FY26 EBITDA margin contracted 70 bps to 8.5% from 9.2%

Consumer Durables margin collapsed to 1.6% vs 4.2% in FY25, segment struggling

Capex exceeded guidance by ₹49-60 crore, higher than planned

GST show cause notice of ₹17.75 crore, IT demand ₹10.21 crore, customs duty ₹2.13 crore - total
contingent liabilities ~₹30 crore

Revenue growth of 7.0% FY YoY below the 15-20% CAGR needed for ₹9,000-10,000 crore target by FY30

Raw materials cost declined but other expenses rose 11.6% YoY indicating cost pressure in selling/distribution

items.title: DIXON delivered strong FY26 with 25.76% revenue growth to ₹4,887 Cr and robust cash generation (FCF ₹715 Cr, CFO/PAT 1.08x). However, Q4 saw a 35.9%...
items.description_html:
DIXON delivered strong FY26 with 25.76% revenue growth to ₹4,887 Cr and robust cash generation (FCF ₹715 Cr, CFO/PAT 1.08x). However, Q4 saw a 35.9% PAT decline due to the absence of prior year's exceptional gain, and operating margin compressed 124 bps missing expansion guidance.

The company's heavy capex cycle (₹1,068 Cr) and backward integration through Q Tech acquisition position it for future margin improvement, but near-term execution remains mixed with PLI scheme uncertainty pending.

Positive Factors

FY26 revenue grew 25.76% YoY to ₹4,887 Cr, exceeding management's strong growth target

Operating cash flow of ₹1,782 Cr with CFO/PAT ratio of 1.08x indicates high earnings quality

Free cash flow of ₹715 Cr positive despite heavy capex, up 240% YoY

Capex of ₹1,068 Cr delivered capacity expansion at Noida, Tirupati, Chennai and Q Tech acquisition

Company EBITDA margin expanded 131 bps to 5.25% including Other Income

Working capital remained negative at -7 days, a competitive advantage

Finance costs declined 10.94% YoY despite borrowings increasing 131% to ₹468 Cr

Risk Factors

Q4 PAT declined 35.9% YoY to ₹298 Cr due to absence of exceptional gain in Q4 FY25

Operating EBITDA margin compressed 124 bps YoY to 3.86%, missing guidance of 120-130 bps expansion

PLI income of ₹1,110 Cr recognized but pending disbursement; scheme expires March 2026

Depreciation grew 39.77% YoY, significantly outpacing revenue growth due to aggressive capex

NCI contribution increased to 12.5% of PAT from 11.1%, diluting owner earnings

Revenue growth decelerated to 2.1% YoY in Q4 from strong earlier quarters

Effectively, it means Iran sees the SoH as an area about 200 kilometers to each side of strait's apex, significantly larger than in the past.

items.title: Berger Paints delivered a strong Q4 with EBITDA margin of 18.3% (standalone) exceeding management's 15-17% guidance range and volume growth of 11.8% s...
items.description_html:
Berger Paints delivered a strong Q4 with EBITDA margin of 18.3% (standalone) exceeding management's 15-17% guidance range and volume growth of 11.8% surpassing the ~10% target. PAT grew 27.5% YoY demonstrating strong operating leverage despite raw material cost pressures.

The 10-quarter high gross margin of 42.3% and accelerating volume growth (11.8% vs 8% in Q3) indicate a sustained margin inflection. Price increases of over 11% from March 2026 and strong demand recovery in urban markets provide near-term catalysts. The debt-free balance sheet and strong cash conversion (1.28x) support continued execution.

Positive Factors

Q4 Standalone EBITDA margin 18.3% exceeded 15-17% guidance range by 130-330 bps

Q4 volume growth 11.8% exceeded management guidance of ~10% by 180 bps

PAT grew 27.5% YoY to ₹335.25 crore, significantly outpacing revenue growth of 6.1%

Gross margin at 42.3% is a 10-quarter high, indicating sustained margin improvement

Operating cash flow of ₹1,406.43 crore represents 1.28x cash conversion quality

Company maintains debt-free balance sheet with Debt/Equity at 0.02x

Volume growth accelerated from 8% in Q3 to 11.8% in Q4 showing demand momentum

Subsidiaries and JVs delivered double-digit growth in topline and operating profit

Risk Factors

Raw material costs grew 7.7% YoY vs revenue growth 6.1%, indicating input price pressure

Total expenses grew 8.4% YoY vs revenue 6.1%, causing margin compression at operating level

Inventory build of ₹180.83 crore in Q4 provides temporary margin boost but may reverse

FY26 PAT declined 4.6% YoY to ₹1,128.02 crore despite Q4 strength

Subsidiary contribution to PAT is modest at 2.4%, limiting diversification benefit

Nepal operations impacted by political instability affecting regional performance

items.title: RT Maheshwer Peri: It isnt surprising that NEET exam has been cancelled by the @NTA_Exams . The hub of this entire operation is Sikar, where the succe...
items.description_html:
RT Maheshwer Peri
It isnt surprising that NEET exam has been cancelled by the @NTA_Exams . The hub of this entire operation is Sikar, where the success rate is 6X of national average. A similar thing happened in 2024 too, but the allegations were brushed under the carpet. Had we dealt with it in 2024 with an iron hand, this wouldnt have repeated.

What we know is that 140 of the 180 questions in NEET were part of a guess paper of 410 questions. What is alarming is that the order of the questions and the options were exactly the same.

The modus operandi is simple. In Sikar, the students are called in for a Mock test, a day before the actual exam and made to prepare for each of the questions in the guess paper. The students had 140 of the 180 questions preepared, thus guaranteed 600 of the 720 marks even before they entered the exam hall.

Time to go after the paper leakers. Jail them. 23 Lac students and families will pay the price of the brushing under the carpet we did in 2024. @careers360

items.title: RT Rajeev Mantri: 1/ Owing to high barriers to entry, India’s lodging and hotels industry has become a cartel that raises prices at will and fleeces ...
items.description_html:
RT Rajeev Mantri
1/ Owing to high barriers to entry, India’s lodging and hotels industry has become a cartel that raises prices at will and fleeces customers.

India’s luxury hotel average daily rate (ADR) in Mumbai and Delhi now rival those of New York and London. Absurdly enough, the financial press sometimes celebrates this as some triumph of Indian hospitality. It is nothing of the sort. High prices are the unmistakable stamp of a supply-constrained market in a low income country, which should actually have a very competitively priced and deep pool of lodging options. The hotel industry is a structural failure being hyped as a success.

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Macquarie on SBI O-P, TP Rs 1150 4Q FY26: Earnings miss driven by weak margins and trading losses NIM impacted by higher proportion of...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Macquarie on SBI
O-P, TP Rs 1150
4Q FY26: Earnings miss driven by weak margins and trading losses
NIM impacted by higher proportion of floating book
Asset quality remains a bright spot
Stock trades at 1.6x FY27E P/BV, which appears comfortable given expected ROEs of 15%.

JPM on SBI
OW, TP 1225
SBI reported a weaker than expected 4Q with NII (+3.8% y/y, -2% q/q) at Rs 443.8bn moderating vs +9% y/y in 3Q26 and coming 6% below JPM.
SBI recorded a 17bps q/q decline in NIM (whole bank) to 2.81% and an 18bps q/q decline in NIM (domestic) to 2.93%, vs. Street expectations of flattish to marginally lower margins q/q. However, SBI remains confident of NIM recovery in FY27 vs 4Q backed by multiple steps highlighted below.
Asset quality was benign with credit costs (on avg loans) at 24bps (-16bps q/q), although slippages (+3bps q/q) edged up slightly on seasonality.

MS on SBI
EW, TP Rs 980
Focus on call was on sharp NIM decline.
Management expects some recovery in NIM from 4Q level driven largely by loan yield recovery
Assume some recovery, yet cut F27- 28 NIM forecast by >20bp
Trim F27 & F28 EPS forecasts by 4% & 2%
Management guided to FY27 loan growth of 13-15%, assuming no material macro deterioration

CLSA on SBI
O-P TP Rs 1275
Reported a 4% beat on its 4QFY26 PBT driven by lower-than-expected opex and credit costs & a higher recovery from written-off accounts.
However, on top line, it was a weak quarter, with NII missing estimates by 5%.
This was driven by a 17bp sequential compression in NIM, sharper than expected
Calculated loan yields dropped 40bp to 7.5% driven by repo rate cuts, MCLR repricing & an increase in EBLR share.
Apart from NIM, it was a good quarter.
Loan growth of 17% YoY was best in class, while deposit growth picked up from 9% to 11% YoY
CASA ratio was steady YoY at 39%.
Slippages were steady at 0.5%, & credit costs were 0.2%.

BoFA Sec on SBI
Buy, TP Rs 1200
PAT growth of 6% YoY led by strong loan growth and lower credit cost offset by NIM compression and lower non‑interest income
NIM contraction led by repo‑linked loan repricing, MCLR resets post rate cuts, and mix shift towards T‑bill‑linked loans
FY27 guidance - 13-15% loan growth, full year NIM >3% , <50 bps credit cost and 1%+ RoA

Bernstein on SBI
Market Perform, TP Rs 1300
SBI delivered a mixed 4Q26
Loan growth accelerated to 17% YoY but NIM saw a sharp 18 bps QoQ decline to 2.93%, steepest compression amongst large banks.
Slippages rose sequentially, though largely seasonal, while headline GNPA improved to 1.49%.
RoA moderated 12 bps QoQ to 1.07%, weighed down by NIM compression & a Treasury MTM loss.
Management retained FY27 guidance of 13–15% credit growth, >3% domestic NIM & 50 bps credit costs
Management reaffirmed its through-cycle commitment to 1%+ RoA & 15%+ RoE

UBS on SBI
Neutral, TP Rs 1080
NII declined QoQ as margins dip 18bp QoQ due to loan pricing cut
Loan growth at c5% QoQ led by Corporates; asset quality stable
Management guides for loan growth of 13-15% for FY27; credit cost of c50bp
Expect bank's ROA to see downward pressure as treasury gains and credit costs normalize while margin trends remain subdued
Thus estimate ROA of 0.95% over FY27-28E (vs c1.12% in FY26) & believe risk reward at current valuations (1.2x FY27E P/BV) seems fair
Kotak Inst Eqt on SBI

Buy, TP Rs 1250
SBI reported about 5% yoy earnings growth, led by a 55% yoy decline in provisions, while operating profits declined about 10% yoy
Bank reported solid loan growth (~17% yoy), low credit costs (~25 bps) and a NIM decline of 20 bps qoq
Miss against expectations is likely to prompt a near-term pause in its re-rating, with investors awaiting evidence of recovery before rerating bank.

items.title: Lots of countries announced measures early on to deal with the shortages/high prices due to #IranWar. India had earlier invoked the Essential Commodit...
items.description_html:
Lots of countries announced measures early on to deal with the shortages/high prices due to #IranWar.

India had earlier invoked the Essential Commodities Act to protect & control low LPG supply.

A (non-exhaustive) list of what others have done,

1. Philippines: Declared a state of national emergency due to the imminent danger of critically low energy supply.

2. Bangladesh: Daily fuel purchase limits by vehicle type. For eg - bikes up to 2 litres, pvt cars 10 litres etc. Shorter govt office hours. Early closure of markets & malls. And more.

3. S. Korea: Tightened public-sector vehicle rotation from five days to two days; vehicles restricted every alternate business day due to low fuel supply.

4. Indonesia: Announced fuel rationing; mandated civil servants to work from home once a week amid fuel supply disruptions and petrol-station shortages.

5. Australia: Sought agreements with Asian trade partners to secure refined fuel in return for LNG, as hundreds of service stations ran out of fuel.

6. Slovenia: Set a daily cap on road-fuel purchases at gas stations due to shortages at the pump.

#Nifty #GIFTNIFTY #BankNifty #India #IranWar‌ #Oil #Gold

items.title: RT Anthony DiGiorgio, DO, MHA: This is the ultimate midwit healthcare take. No, 32 countries have not “figured out” universal healthcare. The UK has...
items.description_html:
RT Anthony DiGiorgio, DO, MHA
This is the ultimate midwit healthcare take.

No, 32 countries have not “figured out” universal healthcare.

The UK has “free” healthcare, and roughly 1 in 3 cancer patients in England still fail to start treatment within 62 days of urgent referral.

Canada has “free” healthcare, and the median wait for neurosurgical treatment is around a year.

Australia has “free” healthcare, and over half the country still buys private insurance despite paying for a public universal system with their taxes.

Switzerland has universal coverage, because residents are required to buy private insurance. There is no government system where benevolent bureaucrats tuck you in at night with a warm blanket and an MRI appointment.

The actual lesson from other wealthy countries is not “they figured it out.”

America’s system has huge problems. Our prices are insane, insurance markets are distorted, and hospital systems are cartelized. Our regulations make care more expensive than it needs to be.

Yet we still guarantee access to even the 8% who don’t have coverage. We give easy routes to qualify for medicaid for those with disabilities.

Pretending the rest of the world solved healthcare because they slapped the word “universal” on a rationing scheme is not analysis.

It is bumper sticker policy for people who think access means having a card in your wallet while you wait a year to see the doctor you need.


daz: Free Universal Healthcare is so complicated and expensive that only 32 of the 33 wealthiest countries in the world have figured it out.

items.title: RT Pritesh Gupta: most debates fail before they even begin because the problem is framed wrong from the start. people think they are arguing about sol...
items.description_html:
RT Pritesh Gupta
most debates fail before they even begin because the problem is framed wrong from the start.

people think they are arguing about solutions, but often they are only reacting to symptoms. the real issue stays hidden underneath. a good framework helps you move from what is visible to what is actually causing it.

this is why many indian systems remain stuck. the focus stays on inputs: money spent, schemes launched, announcements made. but inputs are not outcomes. distributing tablets is not the same as children learning. activity gets measured because it is easy to count. outcomes get ignored because they are harder to see.

whoever defines the problem usually controls the solution. frame a learning crisis as an infrastructure issue and the answer becomes buildings and devices. frame it as an information issue and the answer becomes better data, feedback, and accountability. same reality, different response.

that is why framing is not just intellectual, it is political. the way a problem is named decides who gets blamed, what progress looks like, and whose reality matters. which is why getting the question right is often harder, and far more important, than finding an answer.

check out: http://frameworks.pranaykotas.com by @pranaykotas

items.title: RT nikhilv: There are a few reasons for this - Inequality literature in Claude's pretraining data is 'actionable', the model sees this as a problem to...
items.description_html:
RT nikhilv
There are a few reasons for this -
Inequality literature in Claude's pretraining data is 'actionable', the model sees this as a problem to be solved. Conversely, literature around growth is framed descriptively, like matter-of-fact. So the model has learnt that anyone serious about public policy will be more concerned about inequality than growth.
Anthropic's constitution is also to blame - it introduces priors, some explicitly mentioned (avoid harms to vulnerable groups/be fair etc - inequality concerns follow naturally). And then you have RLHF from US/UK left of centre cohorts, and black box reveals itself.
Try grok and you'll see a mirror image - xAI tuned the priors the other way, so it may flip rather than disappear @pranaykotas @srajagopalan @amitvarma @AshishPonders @acorn

items.title: Commodity costs were rising sharply, but Ather still improved margins. EBITDA moved from -23.3% to -2.5% over two years (~2080 bps YoY improvement), e...
items.description_html:
Commodity costs were rising sharply, but Ather still improved margins.

EBITDA moved from -23.3% to -2.5% over two years (~2080 bps YoY improvement), even as:
– Aluminium & metals prices rose ~41%
– Battery and electronics costs remained volatile

What helped:
• Better sourcing & inventory planning
• Use of alternate materials (light rare-earth magnets)
• Shift to LFP batteries
• Product design changes

A good example of how execution can offset cost pressures.

items.title: RT Tijori: The company is Ajinomoto Co. (TYO: 2802), and the product that matters here is Ajinomoto Build-up Film, or ABF. What makes this interesting...
items.description_html:
RT Tijori
The company is Ajinomoto Co. (TYO: 2802), and the product that matters here is Ajinomoto Build-up Film, or ABF.

What makes this interesting is the switching-cost moat. ABF is a thermosetting film that has to maintain sub-micron flatness across a 600mm sheet, and every single layer needs to be defect-free because one flaw means you scrap the entire 20-layer substrate carrying a $40,000 GPU die. Ajinomoto spent three decades dialing in the exact resin-filler chemistry, controls both the IP and the precursor amino-acid feedstock, and qualifying a new film with TSMC, Intel, or Samsung takes five to seven years of joint testing. So even if Sekisui or a Chinese competitor cracked the chemistry tomorrow, no chipmaker is going to risk a yield disaster just to switch suppliers.

The cleaner play here isn't actually Ajinomoto itself — the food business still accounts for roughly 70% of revenue, which dilutes the AI thesis and is exactly why Palliser has been pushing for a spin-off. If you want pure-play exposure, look downstream at the ABF substrate manufacturers: Unimicron, Nan Ya PCB, Kinsus, Ibiden, Shinko Electric, and AT&S.


SemiAnalysis: There is a single thin-film material required by every AI chip on earth. GPUs, TPUs, custom ASICs. All of them. 98% of global supply controlled by one Japanese chemical company. Zero production-ready alternatives. One producer fully booked through 2027. Raising prices. Lead times

items.title: The company is Ajinomoto Co. (TYO: 2802), and the product that matters here is Ajinomoto Build-up Film, or ABF. What makes this interesting is the swi...
items.description_html:
The company is Ajinomoto Co. (TYO: 2802), and the product that matters here is Ajinomoto Build-up Film, or ABF.

What makes this interesting is the switching-cost moat. ABF is a thermosetting film that has to maintain sub-micron flatness across a 600mm sheet, and every single layer needs to be defect-free because one flaw means you scrap the entire 20-layer substrate carrying a $40,000 GPU die. Ajinomoto spent three decades dialing in the exact resin-filler chemistry, controls both the IP and the precursor amino-acid feedstock, and qualifying a new film with TSMC, Intel, or Samsung takes five to seven years of joint testing. So even if Sekisui or a Chinese competitor cracked the chemistry tomorrow, no chipmaker is going to risk a yield disaster just to switch suppliers.

The cleaner play here isn't actually Ajinomoto itself — the food business still accounts for roughly 70% of revenue, which dilutes the AI thesis and is exactly why Palliser has been pushing for a spin-off. If you want pure-play exposure, look downstream at the ABF substrate manufacturers: Unimicron, Nan Ya PCB, Kinsus, Ibiden, Shinko Electric, and AT&S.

Panel on Global Growth Prospects: Looking Forward

The world went through a difficult time after the pandemic. In 2022, the US started raising rates, and the Ukraine war hit. We are standing at the edge of the unknown.

Chairperson Prof. @ajay_shah at the #AsiaEconomicDialogue.
